coredata.dc:title: Construction and multifunctional applications of carbon dots/PVA nanofibers with phosphorescence and thermally activated delayed fluorescence
coredata.dc:creator: He, Jiangling
He, Youling
Chen, Yonghao
Zhang, Xuejie
Hu, Chaofan
Zhuang, Jianle
Lei, Bingfu
Liu, Yingliang
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescence of carbon dots (CDs) has grown well for substantial applications, but the long-lived photoluminescence, such as room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF), still catch less attention due to the serious deactivation of triplets under ambient conditions, leading to extremely low radiative efficiency. Herein, we simultaneously achieved both of them by incorporating CDs into poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) with the assistance of electrospinning technology. Through systematic investigation, ordered mesoporous structure from the electrospun CDs/PVA nanofibers can effectively stabilize the triplet states of CDs, thus enabling the reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) process for TADF and realizing the first example in CDs/polymer system. RTP is closely related to the triplet excited states of the carbonyls located at the surface of CDs and PVA molecule can rigidify these groups with hydrogen bonds, which can prevent quenching from atmospheric oxygen to promote the RTP and TADF behaviors in the air atmosphere. The afterglow spectrum of these nanofibers exhibits dual emission centered at 456 nm and 569 nm, originated from TADF and RTP, respectively. By changing the excitation wavelength or by adjusting the temperature of measurement, the color of the photoluminescence (PL) can be systematically tailored, the white-light emitting is promising. Moreover, these nanofibers show a long average afterglow lifetime of 1.61 s with visually recognizable period of 9 s. Moreover, CDs/PVA composites (nanofibers, solution, film, and aerogel) demonstrate potentialities in optical imaging, writing, anti-counterfeiting, temperature sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Room temperature phosphorescence
Thermally activated delayed fluorescence
Electrospinning technology

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs/PVA solution for applications
Immobilization of CDs in PVA with electrospinning procedure
Synthesis of spongy CDs/PVA aerogel and CDs/PVA film
Structural and optical characterization of as-prepared CDs
Multifunctional applications of the CDs/PVA composite
CDs/PVA composite for writing, anti-counterfeiting, and used for the film and aerogel morphologies

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The representative TEM image and (b) size distribution of as-prepared CDs. (c) Excitation-dependence fluorescence emission spectra of as-prepared CDs aqueous solution. (d) Excitation spectrum (green line, em: 420 nm) and absorption spectrum of CDs aqueous solution (blue line). (e) and (f) Fluorescence and phosphorescence emission spectra of as-prepared CDs powder under various excitation wavelengths, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure caption, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The SEM images of the CDs/PVA nanofibers at (a) low and (b) high magnification, inset: photograph of synthetic these nanofibers, the dimensions of this sheet is 8 cm 6 cm (l w). (c) and (d) exhibit the representative TEM images of the CDs/PVA nanofibers and the PVA nanofibers, respectively. (e) and (f) represent the further magnified TEM and HRTEM of CDs/PVA nanofibers, respectively.

(a) Emission spectra of the nanofibers under excitation wavelength of 360 nm. (b) Pore-size distribution curves of as-prepared nanofibers. (c) Afterglow emission spectrum of the nanofibers, the aerogel, and the film (ex: 360 nm). (d), (e) and (f) represent the decay curves of fluorescence (under ex: 360 nm and em: 450 nm), RTP (under ex: 360 nm and em: 569 nm), and afterglow (under ex: 360 nm and em: 540 nm) profile at room temperature (300 K), respectively. (g) Phosphorescence-TADF emission spectra of the nanofibers at various temperatures (80–420 K) recorded every 10 K under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. (h) Temperature-dependent integrated intensity from 480–710 nm to 415–480 nm.

(a) CDs/PVA films with various mass ratio (0.01%, 0.1%, 0.2%, 0.5%, 1.0%, 2.0%, 3.0%, and 4.0%) under day-light (up) and UV-light (bottom). (b) and (c) represent the corresponding fluorescence emission spectra and RTP emission spectra (ex: 340 nm), respectively.

(a) CDs/PVA aerogels with various mass ratio (0.01%, 0.1%, 0.2%, 0.5%, 1.0%, 2.0%, 3.0%, and 4.0%) under day-light (up) and UV-light (bottom). (b) and (c) represent the corresponding fluorescence emission spectra and RTP emission spectra (ex: 340 nm), respectively.

(a) Confocal microscopic images of CDs solution. (b) Electrospun PVA nanofibers without composited with CDs. (c) Electrospun CDs/PVA nanofibers. (d), (e), and (f) Fluorescence emission spectra captured at excitation wavelength of 365 nm, 488 nm, and 543 nm for CDs solution, nanofibers without CDs, and nanofibers with CDs, respectively.

(a) Handwritten characters CDs/PVA, electrospinning, fluorescence, phosphorescence, delayed fluorescence on a paper using CDs/PVA solution as ink. (b) Performance of this solution for anti-counterfeiting ink applied in commodity trademark. (c) Performance of this solution for anti-counterfeiting ink applied in the fingerprint identification, under daylight, UV-365 nm (ON), and after turning off the UV lamp respectively from left to right. (d) This solution in a Petri dish after 2 days at the room temperature to obtain the CDs/PVA complex film, and is cut into fragments, (e) Photographs of spongy CDs/PVA composite, (f) photographs of as-prepared CDs/PVA nanofibers under daylight, UV-365 nm (ON), and after turning off the UV lamp respectively from left to right, respectively.
Abbreviations for some chemicals and items.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: CDs/PVA composites correspond to CDs/PVA film, CDs/PVA aerogel, and electrospun CDs/PVA nanofiber.
Nanofiber is shorten for electrospun CDs/PVA nanofiber.

head.ce:title: Construction and multifunctional applications of carbon dots/PVA nanofibers with phosphorescence and thermally activated delayed fluorescence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optimal Agricultural, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guang Zhou 510642, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optimal Agricultural

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence of carbon dots (CDs) has grown well for substantial applications, but the long-lived photoluminescence, such as room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF), still catch less attention due to the serious deactivation of triplets under ambient conditions, leading to extremely low radiative efficiency. Herein, we simultaneously achieved both of them by incorporating CDs into poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) with the assistance of electrospinning technology. Through systematic investigation, ordered mesoporous structure from the electrospun CDs/PVA nanofibers can effectively stabilize the triplet states of CDs, thus enabling the reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) process for TADF and realizing the first example in CDs/polymer system. RTP is closely related to the triplet excited states of the carbonyls located at the surface of CDs and PVA molecule can rigidify these groups with hydrogen bonds, which can prevent quenching from atmospheric oxygen to promote the RTP and TADF behaviors in the air atmosphere. The afterglow spectrum of these nanofibers exhibits dual emission centered at 456 nm and 569 nm, originated from TADF and RTP, respectively. By changing the excitation wavelength or by adjusting the temperature of measurement, the color of the photoluminescence (PL) can be systematically tailored, the white-light emitting is promising. Moreover, these nanofibers show a long average afterglow lifetime of 1.61 s with visually recognizable period of 9 s. Moreover, CDs/PVA composites (nanofibers, solution, film, and aerogel) demonstrate potentialities in optical imaging, writing, anti-counterfeiting, temperature sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence has been greatly documented and studied, and is also the most ordinary and widespread optical property of carbon dots (CDs)-based materials [1–6]. The room temperature phosphorescence (RTP), which is the emission from triplet excited states by way of the efficient intersystem crossing (ISC) of singlet to triplet, or which is that when the incident light stop, photoluminescence can persist for some time in room temperature. To date, RTP has displayed tremendous potentialities in light-emitting devices, organic electronics, sensing, and biological imaging. Observed phosphorescence generally comes from inorganics or heavy metal-containing complexes [7–11], which usually are expensive, cytotoxic, and/or observable only at low temperature or in the crystalline forms [12]. To prepare the nano-RTP or organic materials is still challenging and is actually difficult to accomplish [13–15].This is partially because the spin-orbit coupling of electrons in pure organic compounds is so frail that it cannot assist favourable ISC [16]. RTP process concerns spin-forbidden transition, largely blamed for low probability of radiative pathway and ISC between singlet state and triplet state [17]. Moreover, oxygen can quench triplets easily owning to its ground state is triplet [18]. Importantly, in 2013, Zhao et al. firstly shared RTP phenomenon based on water soluble CDs and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [16], then several CDs-based RTP materials have been developed [19–24]. Significantly, fixation and rigidification in a solid-state matrix with the assistance of hydrogen bonding are usually needed to inhibit nonradiative decay of the excited triplet states and prevent quenching from atmospheric oxygen [16,25]. The thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) exhibits the ability to reap triplet excited state for the efficient reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) coming from the triplet to singlet excited states [26–29]. To date, most of the reported TADF materials are from organic molecules or metal-organic complexes [30–32] rather than CDs-based composites. So far there are only two reports about TADF from CD-based materials [25,26]. They are the strategies for facilely preparing TADF materials via covalently fixing CDs onto SiO2 (named m-CDs@nSiO2) [25] and confining CDs in zeolitic matrices [26], respectively. Both of them can be attributed to CDs/inorganic system. There is no report of TADF realized in CDs/polymer system. This is actually a great challenge because of the intrinsic limitations for producing advantageous TDAF emission. New TADF materials are highly desired to develop the multifunctional applications.

Electrospinning is widely believed to be a versatile and significant method for constructing the long polymer fiber with a diameter ranging from nanometers to several micrometers [33–35]. As for this technique, a high voltage needs to be implemented to a conductive capillary connected to a reservoir, which contains the polymer solution, where solvents should be carefully selected, polymers can form composite nanofibers. Its characteristic merits can uniquely accomplish the rapid fabrication of CDs associated with polymers, resulting in the formation of microfibers along with large surface areas to improve the sensing efficiency [33,36], and is also an excellent means to prepare mesoporous structure.

Via this technology, many contributive applications of this CDs/PVA composite can be realized. Counterfeiting is a serious trouble for threatening companies, customers, and governments with the progress of the society [37–39]. Therefore, anti-counterfeiting is urgently needed for protecting significant and estimable items, such as logos, brands, fingerprint, and banknotes. During the last decades, some securities measures have been applied in mitigating the counterfeiting. These measures evolve from the ordinary hologram, watermark, metal thread to electronic tracking, and spectroscopic analysis [40–44]. But the present techniques are relatively conversant to falsifiers. Accordingly, it is essential to develop the sophisticated materials for security. Fortunately, luminescent materials offer advantages in high throughput, easy handling, facile design as well, and are thus favourably used in anti-counterfeiting. Moreover, temperature is the basic parameter in the field of science and technology. Optical temperature determination shows the excellences of being inherently parallel and noninvasive, making it outstanding among other methods. Detailedly, CDs-based luminescent temperature sensors from photoluminescent (PL) materials have attracted much attention [45–48], notably, both RTP and TADF simultaneously used for temperature sensing indicate more advantages than fluorescence, because RTP and TADF are quite more sensitive to the change of temperature than fluorescence.

In this work, we discuss a concise and effective method to generate polymer nanofibers containing embeded CDs, which are the electrospun CDs/PVA nanofibers, simultaneously exhibiting RTP and TADF phenomena. Moreover, both TADF and RTP promote the formation of afterglow emission. These nanofibers are with no heavy atoms such as halogens and metals for minimizing nonradiative deactivation. Herein, we focus on discussion about RTP and TADF from these nanofibers and their performance in optical imaging, anti-counterfeiting, and temperature sensing. Meanwhile, we have shaped CDs/PVA composite into film and aerogel, expanding more potential applications.

Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared using hydrothermal method in accordance with the reported procedure [49] with some modifications. Specifically, PVA (1 g) was dissolved in deionized water (20 mL) at 80 °C before mixing with EDA (0.75 mL). Then the mixture was delivered to a Teflon autoclave (volume of 30 mL) for a hydrothermal treatment at 220 °C for 10 h. After that reaction, a certain amount of acetone was introduced into the as-obtained brown suspension to precipitate excess PVA. The precipitation was removed by centrifugation process (18000 rpm, 10 min) and the obtained transparent solution was further purified by dialysis (the molecular weight cut off (MWCO) is 1000 Da) in deionized water, then CDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs/PVA solution for applications
ce:section.ce:para: 10 mL CDs solution (1 g/L) was transferred into a 50 mL three-necked flask under magnetic stirring, followed by addition of PVA (1 g) when it was heated to 80 °C. The mixture should be kept heating and stirring for 2 h. Then obtained CDs/PVA solution was naturally cooled to the room temperature for further process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization of CDs in PVA with electrospinning procedure
ce:section.ce:para: PVA (1 g) dissolved in CDs solution (10 mL, 1 g/L) was used as the precursor to fabricate CDs/PVA nanofibers by a custom-made electrospinning machine [50–52]. The voltage (15 kV) was used between a grounded aluminium foil collector and a droplet of this kind solution resting on the end of the needle syringe. The syringe containing the viscous solution was tied up horizontally through using a needle-collector with distance about 15 cm. The flow rate (0.8 mL/h) was applied in the electrospinning process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of spongy CDs/PVA aerogel and CDs/PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: The same precursor was used to fabricate CDs/PVA aerogel by freeze-drying and to mould CDs/PVA film by drying at 60 °C.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were performed on a TECNAI 10 (PHILIPS) electron microscope. The UV–vis absorption spectrum was gathered with UV/Vis spectrometer UV-2550 purchased from Shimadzu company of Japan. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were recorded by the Avatar 360 FT-IR (Thermo Nicolet) spectrometer. Both photoluminescent emission spectra and afterglow decay profiles were measured on F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer. Absolute quantum yield (QY), fluorescence, phosphorescence were measured using an Edinburg FLS980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hamamatsu, Japan). The electrospun samples were carried out by TL-01 made in Shenzhen (China). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images could be carried out using a XL-30-ESEM (FEI). The bright and fluorescence microscopy images were gathered in the particular confocal laser scanning microscope with mode of LEICA DM 5000 B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and optical characterization of as-prepared CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image as shown in Fig. 1 a and the particle size distribution (Fig. 1b) reveal that the as-prepared CDs are uniform with size distribution mainly ranging from 7 nm to 9 nm. The FTIR result of CDs is given in Fig. S1. The photoluminescent (PL) spectra exhibit an emission maximum at 420 nm when excited at 340 nm, and display the obvious ex-dependence property of the emission wavelengths and intensities, which is an unifying PL character of CDs (Fig. 1c) [53]. The quantum yields of as-prepared solid-state CDs, the nanofibers, the film, and the aerogel were 10.29%, 22.57%, 20.29%, and 20.79%, respectively, as shown in Table S1.

These quantum yields of CDs-based composites are comparable to previous reports [51,53]. To investigate thoroughly the origin of PL, we compared the absorption spectrum and the excitation spectrum of CDs diluted in water (Fig. 1d). There is a peak at 253 nm and a broad band centred at 350 nm, attributing to
π →
π
∗
transition from the CC bonds and
n →
π
∗
transition from the CO bonds, respectively [52,54]. The fluorescence and phosphorescence emission spectra of as-obtained CDs powder under various excitation wavelengths given in Fig. 1e and f, respectively. The CDs powder only exhibited fluorescence and quite weak phosphorescence without showing thermally activated delayed fluorescence. Moreover, dispersing CDs in these PVA nanofibers resulted in bright blue-light emitting under 365 nm-UV excitation wavelength Fig. 7f (left), then showed efficient RTP after photoexcitation was stopped, as shown in Fig. 7f (right).

Fig. 2 a and b show the SEM images of the nanofibers with the diameter in the range of 20–40 nm, demonstrating that they were successfully fabricated via electrospinning technique at 15 kV. Furthermore, the woven nanofiber sheet is white and flexible (inset of Fig. 2a). In addition, since electrospun nanofibers are with small cross-sections (20–40 nm), the composites fabricated using this method can maintain their optical transparency, which can make these nanofibers suitable candidates for a variety of new optoelectronic applications, such as nonlinear optical materials, optical wave guides, and optical filters for the near Vis-UV-vis wavelength region [55]. Moreover, the CDs inlaid in these composite nanofibers can be observed from the TEM images. Comparing the TEM images of PVA nanofibers in the presence and the absence of CDs, where they were demonstrated in Fig. 2c and d, respectively, we could see that CDs were successfully embedded in PVA substrate, which was further approved by Fig. 2e and f. The lattice structure shown in Fig. 2f indicated that CDs were indeed embeded in PVA molecules. And CDs/PVA nanofibers are with similar diameter (30–60 nm) to that of pure PVA nanofibers (40–50 nm). Besides, CDs are embeded uniformly without obvious aggregation, it is to be noted that the prepared composite nanofibers exhibit a highly flexible self-supporting membrane structure that could be easily tailored to the desired shape for a particular applications.

Fig. 3 a exhibits PL spectra of these nanofibers, their fluorescence spectrum (blue line) has emission peak located at 450 nm. And another spectrum shows dual emission located at 456 nm and 569 nm, which could be assigned to TADF and RTP, respectively. Accordingly, these nanofibers provide a small
Δ
E
ST
of 0.0367 eV (
E
S 1
= 2.756
eV for peak at 450 nm;
E
T 1
= 2.719
eV for peak at 456 nm), which is favourable for overcoming competitive non-radiative decay pathways, leading to highly TADF behaviour. Fig. S2 and Fig. 3f depict the afterglow against time curves of these CDs/PVA composites, the afterglow intensity first decreases quickly and then gently, this behaviour is similar to many reports [56]. It is about 9 s that the afterglow of nanofibers permitted being visually recognized (Fig. 3f). Moreover, the pore-size distribution curve of these synthesized nanofibers was shown in Fig. 3b. Narrow and uniform pore-size distribution is reserved in the range of mesoporous size (2–5 nm), this structure can effectively stabilize the triplet states of CDs, thus enabling the RISC process for TADF. Meanwhile, these mesoporous fibers can also hinder oxygen quenching to preserve TADF behaviour at air atmosphere. Contrarily, both the aerogel and the film failed to show this mesoporous property, pore-size for this aerogel is with quite wide distribution (Fig. S3), and the film is nearly with no open-framework structure. This speculation can be further confirmed from their PL spectra. The further assembled films and aerogels with mass ratio (CDs to PVA) distribution of 0.01%, 0.1%, 0.2%, 0.5%, 1.0%, 2.0%, 3.0% and 4.0% were presented in Figs. 4 and 5
, respectively. Fig. 4a indicated the series of films emitting blue light under UV light, catering to the corresponding fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 4b). All of these films exhibit green RTP emission as shown in Fig. 4c. As for various aerogels (Fig. 5a), the fluorescence and RTP spectra of them are given in Fig. 5b and c, respectively. These spectra are closely to the PL spectra of films, revealing blue fluorescence and green RTP, respectively. All these spectra unfavourably show the occurrence of TADF, indicating that the mesoporous structure is favourable for the generation of TADF. More series of this kind electrospun nanofibers were prepared through adjusting voltage or reception distance to confirm that result. As shown in Fig. S4a, we collected nanofibers with the reception distance of 15 cm and changing voltage from 18 V to 22 V. Simultaneously, we controlled voltage at 18 V and adjusted reception distances from 16 cm to 18 cm as given in Fig. S4c to prepare nanofibers. Their corresponding PL emission spectra were presented in Fig. S4b and S4d respectively, in which the PL intensity is closely related to the mesoporous structure, when the mesoporous structure exhibits higher distribution density and higher volume voltage, the PL intensity is stronger.

The phosphorescence and TADF can also be approved from temperature-dependent spectra (Fig. 3g), the PL intensity of peaks located 452 nm and 561 nm are observed to increase and decrease gradually with the temperature gradually changing from 80 K to 420 K respectively, confirming that the existence of a thermally assisted process [57]. Because the emission intensity of peak located 561 nm decreases very evidently as the temperature increases, indicating the promotion of non-radiative decay from triplet (T1) state. Conversely, the emission intensity of peak located 452 nm increases monotonically with the temperature increasing because RISC becomes the rate-determining step, which is typical TADF emitters [57]. Therefore, it could be ascribed the observed two emission peaks of 452 nm and 561 nm to TADF and phosphorescence, respectively.

In order to obtain better insight into the afterglow characteristics (Fig. 3c) of these nanofibers, the afterglow spectrum showed dual emission with CIE coordinate at (0.356, 0.471), corresponding to green light, which can be also approved from Fig. 7f, the green-light afterglow of these nanofibers can be visually observed for several seconds by naked eyes when UV lamp was turned off. But the aerogel and the film failed to present dual emission, only the peak from RTP emission has been observed. However, these nanofibers can perfectly emerge both TADF and RTP. Fluorescence, RTP, and TADF lifetimes were also measured. As expected, these nanofibers express nanoseconds level fluorescence lifetime (13.01 ns, Fig. 3d) by double-exponential function, and subtle level RTP lifetime (Fig. 3e), which nicely fits with tri-exponential function with characteristic lifetime
0.14
μ s (
τ
1
)
,
0.74
μ s (
τ
2
)
and
7.98
μ s (
τ
3
)
, and the average lifetime can be calculated to be
6.81
μ s
. However, a long afterglow lifetime of these nanofibers is confirmed by their decay curve (Fig. 3f), where we can use a double-exponential function curve to fit. This result implies that there are two decay channels to be mainly responsible for the observed afterglow emission [25], and the average lifetime was thus calculated to be 1.61 s.

We further studied the fluorescence and phosphorescence decay curves at various temperatures (Figs. S5 and S6), both of them satisfy the double-exponential and tri-exponential function, respectively. Primary studies show that the RTP was closely related to the triplet excited states of aromatic carbonyls located at the surface of CDs [16] and the PVA molecule can effectively protect their energy from vibrational or rotational loss through rigidifying these groups with the assistance of hydrogen bonds. Meanwhile, the origin of PL phenomena is quite closely related to specific CDs, and the formation diagram of embedding CDs into PVA molecules is presented in Scheme S1 via electrospinning technology.

Furthermore, we also studied the RTP emission spectra of these CDs/PVA composites under different excitation wavelengths from 320 nm to 400 nm as given in Fig. S7. Their emission peaks show slight red-shift when excitation wavelength gradually increased. Accomplishment of such PL material can well realize significance in many valuable fields, for example, temperature sensing and advanced anti-counterfeiting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multifunctional applications of the CDs/PVA composite

ce:section.ce:para:
In order to deeply investigate the potential application of these nanofibers, we tested the PL intensity with the change of temperature as given in Fig. 3g. Fig. 3h plots the integrated intensity ratio of 480–710 nm (A2, phosphorescence) to 415–480 nm (A1, TADF) versus temperature (80–420 K), and a Gaussian fitted relationship was obtained from 200 K to 420 K with a good linearity, which can be described as:
(1)
A
1
/
A
2
= 16.916 - 0.04296 T
or
(2)
T = 393.76 - 23.277
A
2
/
A
1
its correlation coefficient is about 0.9884, where T represents the temperature (K) of these nanofibers. As for this ratiometric PL temperature sensor, its relative sensitivity is followed as:
(3)
S =
∂ (
A
1
/
A
2
) / ∂ T
A
1
/
A
2
where A1 and A2 represent the two integrated intensity of the corresponding dual emissions. Therefore, the sensitivity of this thermometer can be actualized to the optimal value of about
7.26 %
K
- 1
when temperature is 380 K. A linear dependence can be obtained for this thermometer ranging from the low temperature to the middle-high temperature, making these nanofibers a more useful and reliable luminescent thermometer. So we can conclude that these nanofibers can be availably used as temperature sensor.

The confocal microscopic images of as-prepared CDs solution, PVA nanofibers, and CDs/PVA nanofibers were gathered under three different lasers as shown in Fig. 6 . It was evident that blue-, green- and red-light emissions were recorded and identified from that CDs sample solution when excited by 365 nm (laser), 488 nm (laser), and 543 nm (laser), respectively (Fig. 6a), proving the consistency with measured PL emission spectra [49]. There was found dark to display in electrospun PVA nanofibers in absence of CDs given in Fig. 6b. However, blue-, green- and red-light emission were recorded and detected from PVA nanofibers embeded with CDs (the electrospun CDs/PVA nanofibers) as given in Fig. 6c, when they were excited by the laser wavelength of 365 nm, 488 nm and 543 nm, respectively. Their corresponding fluorescence emission spectra under excitation wavelengths of 365 nm, 488 nm, and 543 nm also approved this property as shown in Fig. 6d, e, and f, respectively. Furthermore, this uniformity of the color emitting throughout these nanofibers can confirm the well-distributed CDs without aggregation in the PVA molecules. And the characteristic PL performance of CDs was also perfectly remained in these nanofibers. These nanofibers show the advantage in the field of solid-state optical imaging, compensating for the deficiency in the application of solid-state imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs/PVA composite for writing, anti-counterfeiting, and used for the film and aerogel morphologies
ce:section.ce:para: Besides the long PL lifetime of the CDs/PVA nanofibers, both CDs/PVA film and CDs/PVA aerogel also have long lifetime, detected to be as long as 1.60 s (Fig. S2). We further developed the potential prospects of this CDs-based PL materials as the writing ink, the letters CDs/PVA, electrospinning, fluorescence, phosphorescence, delayed fluorescence were handwritten by CDs/PVA complex solution (Fig. 7 a). Upon excitation with UV-365 nm lamp, these letters showed bright blue color. After cutting off the UV lamp, these letters turned to present green color, which could be clearly perceptible and viewed by the naked eye.

This CDs/PVA composites could also be applied in expanding the niche application in trademark security (Fig. 7b). The number 7 was written on the original brand with colorful light-emitting property under UV excitation, and this handwritten number 7 was transparent and colorless under daylight, so it could greatly be encrypted in this brand. While it would occur and showed blue emission with turning on the UV lamp, then immediately exhibited green-light emission after amputating the UV source. This method was also suitable for fingerprint identification as shown in Fig. 7c. Such exchange in PL emission from blue to green is completely different from the character of emblematical rare-earth-containing afterglow materials, always showing the coincident emitting color after and before the removal of the UV source. This performance can effectively bring these CDs/PVA composites advantages as the optical material for PL ink crime forensics, information security.

Moreover, considering their performance and unique properties, CDs/PVA composite can also be moulded to different shapes, which can effectively meet the various requirements in real applications. Fig. 7d and e exhibit the fabrication of their corresponding film and aerogel state, respectively. The manufactured films are transparent under daylight, they can exhibit blue emission under UV excitation (365 nm), then exhibits green-light emitting after turn off the UV lamp as shown in Fig. 7d. In Fig. 7e, the neoteric spongy aerogel, which is prepared in aqueous system firstly, then with freeze-drying treatment for 10 h, also reflects the same emission properties as film. It has been well researched that the porous structure usually shows merits of adsorbing the specific substance, such as carbon dioxide, oxygen, closely related to the larger surface area than that of the block structure. Therefore, the PL spongy aerogel can be excellently used as a sensor to identify the corresponding molecules, which can successfully reverse the fluorescence emission, RTP or TADF [54]. Hence, the film and aerogel morphologies can reclaim wider application platform for this CDs/PVA composite.

In summary, we herein observed a CDs-based material (electrospun CDs/PVA nanofibers) displaying obvious fluorescence, RTP, TADF simultaneously via electrospinning technology. Further studies revealed that the afterglow spectrum with dual emission is the mixture of TADF and RTP, providing an opportunity to construct white-light afterglow. Up to now TADF is firstly observed in CDs/polymer system. In addition, many new knowledges can be obtained from this study. Mesoporous structure of these nanofibers is conducive to the origination of TADF, carbonyl groups located at the surface of CDs can promote the production of the triplet excitons, which is considered to be an origin of phosphorescence. The rigidity function with hydrogen bonds of PVA is beneficial to the existence of TADF and RTP in the air atmosphere. Furthermore, these nanofibers are with a quite small
Δ
E
ST
, and the electrospinning technology can well preserve the special properties of CDs, and also can convert the flow dynamic CDs/PVA solution to solid state for more convenient application. Moreover, these fabricated nanofibers exhibit excellent property in temperature sensing based on phosphorescence-TADF mode rather than chronic fluorescence mode, the CDs/PVA solution showed great performance in optical imaging and anti-counterfeiting. It is worth mentioning that CDs/PVA composite can be also shaped into film and aerogel, providing more extensive application prospects. (Abbreviations for some chemicals and items has been summarized in Table 1 ).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China [Grant Nos. 21571067, 21401028, 51372091]; the Teamwork Projects Funded by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation [Grant No. S2013030012842]; Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province [Grant Nos. 2014A010105035, 2015B090903074]; and the Guangzhou Science & Technology Project [Grant No. 201605030005]. Special thanks to Prof. Zhou (Wuyi Zhou) for important assistance in electrospinning technology. Sincere thanks to Prof. Wang (Xiaojun Wang) for carefully revising the manuscript.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2018.04.110.
